skills.skill: 10.1 Defining Convergent and Divergent Infinite Series
skills.prompt: This topic covers infinite series fundamentals for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-7.A, Unit 10, Topic 10.1). An infinite series sum[n=1 to infinity] a_n is the limit of its partial sums: S = lim[N->infinity] sum[n=1 to N] a_n. If this limit exists and is finite, the series converges to S; otherwise, it diverges. Key concepts: the sequence of partial sums S_N = a_1 + a_2 + ... + a_N determines convergence; convergence of the series requires S_N to approach a finite limit; divergence means S_N grows without bound or oscillates. Students should distinguish between the sequence {a_n} and the series sum(a_n). A necessary condition for convergence: if sum(a_n) converges, then lim[n->infinity] a_n = 0 (but the converse is false -- the harmonic series is the classic counterexample). This sets the stage for convergence tests in subsequent topics. AP exam note: students should understand partial sums both conceptually and computationally. Vocabulary: infinite series, partial sum, convergent, divergent, sequence, limit, necessary condition.

This topic covers geometric series for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-7.A, Unit 10, Topic 10.2). A geometric series has the form sum[n=0 to infinity] a*r^n = a + ar + ar^2 + ..., where a is the first term and r is the common ratio. The series converges if and only if |r| < 1, and its sum is a/(1-r). If |r| >= 1, the series diverges. Key concepts: identify a and r from the series (the first term and the ratio between consecutive terms); geometric series are one of the few series types with a known exact sum; partial sums of geometric series: S_N = a(1-r^N)/(1-r). Applications include repeating decimals (0.333... = 3/10 + 3/100 + ... = (3/10)/(1-1/10) = 1/3), bouncing ball problems, and present value calculations. Students should recognize geometric series in various forms, including those with different starting indices. AP exam note: geometric series questions are common and test identification of a and r and the convergence condition |r| < 1. Vocabulary: geometric series, common ratio, first term, converges, diverges, sum formula, partial sum.

skills.skill: 10.3 The nth Term Test for Divergence
skills.prompt: This topic covers the nth Term Test (Divergence Test) for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-7.A, Unit 10, Topic 10.3). The nth Term Test states: if lim[n->infinity] a_n != 0 (or does not exist), then sum[n=1 to infinity] a_n diverges. Crucially, this test can only prove divergence, never convergence. If lim[n->infinity] a_n = 0, the test is inconclusive (the series may converge or diverge). The classic example: the harmonic series sum(1/n) diverges even though lim[n->infinity] 1/n = 0. Key concepts: always check the nth Term Test first when analyzing a series; it is the simplest test and quickly identifies many divergent series; the test fails to determine convergence because terms approaching zero can still sum to infinity if they decrease slowly enough. Students should evaluate limits of sequences using L'Hopital's Rule, comparison, or algebraic manipulation. AP exam note: the nth Term Test is often the first step in a convergence/divergence analysis. Students must state that the test is inconclusive when the limit is zero. Vocabulary: nth Term Test, Divergence Test, necessary condition, inconclusive, limit of terms, harmonic series.

This topic covers the Integral Test for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-7.A, Unit 10, Topic 10.4). The Integral Test states: if f is positive, continuous, and decreasing on [1, infinity), and a_n = f(n), then sum[n=1 to infinity] a_n and integral from 1 to infinity of f(x)dx either both converge or both diverge. Key concepts: the test requires verifying three conditions (positive, continuous, decreasing); the test does not give the sum of the series, only whether it converges; the integral and the series do not have the same value. To apply: evaluate the improper integral; if it converges, the series converges; if it diverges, the series diverges. Classic application: the p-series sum(1/n^p) converges if p > 1 and diverges if p <= 1, proved by comparing with integral of 1/x^p. AP exam note: students must verify the conditions of the test when applying it. The Integral Test is especially useful for series involving ln(n) or other functions easily integrated. Vocabulary: Integral Test, positive, continuous, decreasing, improper integral, convergence, divergence, p-series.

This topic covers harmonic and p-series for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-7.A, Unit 10, Topic 10.5). The harmonic series sum[n=1 to infinity] 1/n diverges, despite its terms approaching zero. The p-series sum[n=1 to infinity] 1/n^p converges if p > 1 and diverges if p <= 1. Key examples: sum(1/n) diverges (p = 1, harmonic); sum(1/n^2) converges (p = 2, equals pi^2/6); sum(1/sqrt(n)) = sum(1/n^(1/2)) diverges (p = 1/2 < 1); sum(1/n^3) converges (p = 3). The p-series result, proved via the Integral Test, serves as a benchmark for comparison tests: many series can be compared to p-series to determine convergence. Students should recognize p-series in various forms (including when algebraically rearranged) and know the convergence threshold p = 1. AP exam note: p-series convergence is foundational knowledge tested throughout the series unit. Students should be able to quickly identify the value of p and state convergence/divergence. Vocabulary: harmonic series, p-series, convergence threshold, divergence, comparison benchmark, exponent.

This topic covers the Direct Comparison Test and Limit Comparison Test for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-7.A, Unit 10, Topic 10.6). Direct Comparison: for series with positive terms, if 0 <= a_n <= b_n and sum(b_n) converges, then sum(a_n) converges; if a_n >= b_n >= 0 and sum(b_n) diverges, then sum(a_n) diverges. Limit Comparison: if a_n, b_n > 0 and lim[n->infinity] a_n/b_n = L where 0 < L < infinity, then sum(a_n) and sum(b_n) either both converge or both diverge. Key strategy: choose a comparison series (typically a geometric or p-series) that behaves similarly to the given series for large n. Students should identify the dominant terms for large n to select the comparison series. AP exam note: the Limit Comparison Test is often easier to apply than the Direct Comparison Test. Students must show the comparison series converges/diverges and that the comparison conditions are met. Vocabulary: Direct Comparison Test, Limit Comparison Test, positive terms, inequality, dominant term, p-series, geometric series.

This topic covers the Alternating Series Test (Leibniz Test) for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-7.A, Unit 10, Topic 10.7). An alternating series has the form sum[n=1 to infinity] (-1)^(n+1) * b_n where b_n > 0. The Alternating Series Test states: if (1) b_n is decreasing (b_(n+1) <= b_n for all n) and (2) lim[n->infinity] b_n = 0, then the series converges. Key concepts: the test applies only to alternating series; convergence is conditional (the series of absolute values may still diverge); the partial sums oscillate above and below the limit, getting progressively closer. Classic example: the alternating harmonic series sum((-1)^(n+1)/n) converges (to ln 2), even though the harmonic series diverges. Students should verify both conditions explicitly. AP exam note: students must check both the decreasing condition and the limit condition. The Alternating Series Test is often paired with the error bound (Topic 10.10). Vocabulary: alternating series, Leibniz Test, decreasing, limit zero, conditional convergence, oscillating partial sums.

This topic covers the Ratio Test for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-7.A, Unit 10, Topic 10.8). The Ratio Test states: for sum(a_n) with a_n != 0, compute L = lim[n->infinity] |a_(n+1)/a_n|. If L < 1, the series converges absolutely; if L > 1 (or L = infinity), the series diverges; if L = 1, the test is inconclusive. Key concepts: the Ratio Test is especially effective for series involving factorials (n!), exponentials (r^n), or products of both; it works by comparing the series to a geometric series with ratio L. Classic applications: sum(n!/n^n), sum(x^n/n!), sum(n^2/3^n). For power series, the Ratio Test determines the radius of convergence (Topic 10.13). Students should simplify the ratio a_(n+1)/a_n carefully, canceling common factors. AP exam note: the Ratio Test is one of the most powerful and frequently tested convergence tests. When L = 1, students must use a different test. Vocabulary: Ratio Test, absolute convergence, divergence, inconclusive, factorial, exponential, ratio of consecutive terms.

This topic covers absolute and conditional convergence for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-7.A, Unit 10, Topic 10.9). A series sum(a_n) converges absolutely if sum(|a_n|) converges. It converges conditionally if sum(a_n) converges but sum(|a_n|) diverges. Key theorem: absolute convergence implies convergence (but not vice versa). Testing procedure: (1) check if sum(|a_n|) converges (using any appropriate test for positive series); (2) if yes, the series converges absolutely; (3) if sum(|a_n|) diverges, check if sum(a_n) converges (typically using the Alternating Series Test); (4) if sum(a_n) converges but sum(|a_n|) diverges, the convergence is conditional. Classic example: sum((-1)^n/n) converges conditionally (alternating harmonic converges, but harmonic diverges). AP exam note: students must distinguish between absolute and conditional convergence and test both the original series and the series of absolute values. This classification matters for rearrangement theorems. Vocabulary: absolute convergence, conditional convergence, series of absolute values, rearrangement, alternating, positive series.

This topic covers the Alternating Series Error Bound for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-7.B, Unit 10, Topic 10.10). For a convergent alternating series satisfying the Alternating Series Test conditions, the error in approximating the sum S by the partial sum S_N is bounded by the absolute value of the first omitted term: |S - S_N| <= |a_(N+1)| = b_(N+1). Key concepts: this bound guarantees that using N terms gives an approximation within b_(N+1) of the true sum; the bound is tight (the actual error is at most this value); to achieve a desired accuracy epsilon, find the smallest N such that b_(N+1) < epsilon. Example: for sum((-1)^(n+1)/n), using 4 terms gives error <= 1/5 = 0.2. This error bound is much simpler than bounds for general series. AP exam note: the AP exam frequently asks students to determine how many terms are needed for a given accuracy or to bound the error of a partial sum. Vocabulary: error bound, alternating series, first omitted term, approximation, accuracy, partial sum, truncation error.

skills.skill: 10.11 Finding Taylor Polynomial Approximations of Functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers Taylor polynomials for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-8.A, Unit 10, Topic 10.11). The nth-degree Taylor polynomial of f centered at x = a is P_n(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x-a) + f''(a)(x-a)^2/2! + f'''(a)(x-a)^3/3! + ... + f^(n)(a)(x-a)^n/n!. When a = 0, this is a Maclaurin polynomial. Key concepts: Taylor polynomials approximate f(x) near x = a; higher-degree polynomials generally give better approximations over wider intervals; the polynomial matches f and its first n derivatives at x = a; each term adds one more derivative match. Common Maclaurin polynomials: e^x ~ 1 + x + x^2/2! + ...; sin x ~ x - x^3/3! + x^5/5! - ...; cos x ~ 1 - x^2/2! + x^4/4! - .... Students should compute Taylor polynomials by finding successive derivatives. AP exam note: constructing Taylor polynomials is a core free-response skill. Students must compute derivatives accurately and use the correct formula. Vocabulary: Taylor polynomial, Maclaurin polynomial, center, degree, successive derivatives, approximation, factorial.

This topic covers the Lagrange Error Bound (Taylor's Theorem remainder) for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-8.B, Unit 10, Topic 10.12). The error in approximating f(x) by its nth-degree Taylor polynomial P_n(x) centered at a is bounded by: |R_n(x)| = |f(x) - P_n(x)| <= M * |x-a|^(n+1) / (n+1)!, where M is an upper bound for |f^(n+1)(c)| for all c between a and x. Key concepts: M must bound the (n+1)th derivative on the entire interval between a and x; the error decreases as n increases (for well-behaved functions) and as x gets closer to a; this bound works for any Taylor polynomial, not just alternating series. Students should identify M by finding the maximum of |f^(n+1)| on the relevant interval, which may require knowledge of the function's behavior. AP exam note: Lagrange Error Bound problems require students to identify M correctly and set up the inequality. This is one of the more challenging series topics. Vocabulary: Lagrange Error Bound, remainder, Taylor polynomial, upper bound, (n+1)th derivative, absolute error.

skills.skill: 10.13 Radius and Interval of Convergence of Power Series
skills.prompt: This topic covers the radius and interval of convergence of power series for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-8.C, Unit 10, Topic 10.13). A power series centered at a is sum[n=0 to infinity] c_n (x-a)^n. The radius of convergence R is the value such that the series converges for |x-a| < R and diverges for |x-a| > R (R can be 0, a positive number, or infinity). The interval of convergence is (a-R, a+R) with endpoints tested separately. To find R: use the Ratio Test, computing lim|c_(n+1)/c_n| or lim|c_n|^(1/n) and solving for when the result < 1. At endpoints x = a+R and x = a-R, convergence must be checked individually using other tests (p-series, alternating series, etc.). Key concepts: inside the radius, the series converges absolutely; outside, it diverges; at the endpoints, behavior varies. AP exam note: finding the interval of convergence with endpoint analysis is a classic free-response problem. Students must test both endpoints explicitly. Vocabulary: power series, radius of convergence, interval of convergence, endpoint, Ratio Test, center, absolute convergence.

skills.skill: 10.14 Finding Taylor or Maclaurin Series for a Function
skills.prompt: This topic covers finding Taylor and Maclaurin series for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-8.D, Unit 10, Topic 10.14). The Taylor series of f centered at a is sum[n=0 to infinity] f^(n)(a)/n! * (x-a)^n. The Maclaurin series (a = 0) is sum[n=0 to infinity] f^(n)(0)/n! * x^n. Key known series: e^x = sum(x^n/n!) for all x; sin x = sum((-1)^n x^(2n+1)/(2n+1)!) for all x; cos x = sum((-1)^n x^(2n)/(2n)!) for all x; 1/(1-x) = sum(x^n) for |x| < 1; ln(1+x) = sum((-1)^(n+1) x^n/n) for |x| <= 1 (x != -1). Students should derive series by computing derivatives at the center and finding patterns, or by manipulating known series (substitution, differentiation, integration, multiplication). For example, e^(-x^2) = sum((-1)^n x^(2n)/n!) by substituting -x^2 into the e^x series. AP exam note: students should memorize the common series and use manipulation techniques. Vocabulary: Taylor series, Maclaurin series, known series, manipulation, substitution, pattern, coefficients.

This topic covers representing functions as power series for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-8.E, Unit 10, Topic 10.15). Students use known series and algebraic operations to find power series representations of new functions. Key techniques: (1) substitution into known series (e.g., replace x with -x, 2x, or x^2 in a known series); (2) term-by-term differentiation of a power series to get the series for the derivative; (3) term-by-term integration to get the series for an antiderivative (determine C using initial conditions); (4) multiplying series or adding/subtracting series. For example: integral of e^(-t^2) dt has no elementary antiderivative, but its series representation can be obtained by integrating the series for e^(-t^2) term by term. The interval of convergence may change at endpoints after differentiation or integration. AP exam note: representing functions as power series is a common free-response question. Students should justify convergence of the resulting series. Vocabulary: power series representation, term-by-term differentiation, term-by-term integration, substitution, interval of convergence, manipulation.
